data.title: AI Is Eating Office Jobs
data.description:
The last update for the labor market came last week with the January jobs report that “smashed expectations” but also came with deep revisions for the past year. It was a mixed bag, all told.

The unemployment rate fell to 4.3%, and here's what I had to say about the headline:

The actual jobs report came in way hotter than expected, a good thing, at 135K jobs added over the expected 65K. This continued an upward trend from the last few months, but ultimately is still dwarfed by the hiring pace from a few years ago...

Ultimately, the jobs report was positive...Specific stats that I've been watching are improving, such as involuntary part-time workers and reentrants. Both were a cause for unemployment rising last year, and so it's good news to see them retreating in the first jobs report of 2026...

In the last few days, we've gotten more data to consider, which may give us a clearer picture of where the labor market is headed.

Jobless Claims Data

High-frequency indicators continue to come out, such as initial jobless claims, which are showing a spike above the past few years. However, this could also be temporary, and the trend so far does not indicate the trajectory of the longer-term trend.

Economists like Guy Berger at the Burning Glass Institute are projecting a 1.4% increase over 2025's jobless figures, and so far, that is holding true.

In good news, continuing claims are coming in under projections (which were fairly grim in comparison) and have even come in under the 2025 figure for most of the year so far. While this figure may climb in the coming months if these new initial claims cannot find jobs, the lower level it is sitting at now is a good sign.

This corroborates some of the data we saw on reentrants and long-term unemployment claims (27+ weeks) falling in the Jan. jobs report. Here's an excerpt from the previously mentioned article on that note:

(In 2025), unemployment had been rising primarily because of reentrants to the job market...Workers were being shed out of the employment statistics and into different unemployed categories, like employed part-time for economic reasons.

In Jan., we are seeing a drop in rentrants, as that figure is starting to fade, as well as a drop in both long-term unemployment (over 27 weeks) and unwilling part-time work. This means that these people, some of the most vulnerable in the unemployment picture because they are not job hopping but seeking employment from a position of not having one, are having their conditions improve. We did see a small increase in discouraged workers, but the overall level is still far lower than it was a year ago.

The increase in the “job leaver” unemployment can be considered slightly bullish, as it is unusual to see an increase in people leaving their jobs unless the overall hiring environment is better.

We can see it in this chart tracking the hiring rate going back to 2000. It's at lows we haven't seen since the recovery phase of the Great Financial Crisis (2009-2012).

White-Collar Jobs Are Hit Hard

One of the themes I've been discussing and trying to trade around lately is the idea that AI tools are rapidly becoming able to replace human knowledge workers. I've acknowledged that in the past, I underappreciated the capabilities of these systems. Now, I'm taking them more seriously as a threat to the labor market and believe that while there are certainly jobs that cannot be replaced, there are still many that can be. These businesses have been working on solutions, and while I derided the first attempts they made because they were largely failures, the second and third waves of AI deployments seem to be having better results.

These waves have increasingly been directed at white-collar work.

The trend hit software developers first. It makes sense, since this is one of the tasks that current AI systems are most competent at. They can generate thousands of lines of code in minutes, and so you really only need the decision-makers around, i.e., senior software developers.

I brought up this chart back in September, in “An Economy Not Built For The Young,” but it's worth revisiting here:

We're now starting to see it in the labor data for white-collar workers more broadly, as it's not just the low-hanging fruit of junior developers. The above chart startled me, but this chart scares me.

The absolute level of finance and insurance openings hits multi-decade lows. Information and services at large are also down, but to more historically aligned levels, though this does not account for population growth.

In the time that this chart took place, the U.S. population grew 11%, but white-collar job openings have basically been flat. We've completely reversed the trend started by easy money and stimulus. All of that has been walked back now.

Private Data Is Mixed

Counteracting the official government narrative on jobs, where the data on white-collar jobs is looking bleak, private payrolls are showing absolute levels higher than ten years ago for these same categories of roles.

ADP Research (ADP) has financial activities roles at a 17% gain since 2010, compared to a slight loss from the BLS data. We can see gains in professional services as well, although information jobs have remained essentially flat, up less than 1% in a time when the population grew ~11%.

Although in its most recent release for January, ADP did estimate that a ton of jobs were lost in professional services, around 57,000 fewer employees were on payroll. Information jobs were also down, while financial activities moved at a decent pace, recovering some of their losses from previous months.

Conclusion

New data coming out of the labor market does not make me hopeful that the current unemployment rate of 4.3% will continue to fall from its peak a few months ago at 4.6%. The threat to white-collar workers has never been higher, a problem for the United States' service economy. I expect this pain to continue to reverberate through the labor market, but perhaps not the stock market as quickly—the jobs being lost are not necessarily due to downsizing, but to automation. Historically, that has been good for corporate earnings.

It's not so good for the average worker, who will have to find a new job. That is easier said than done in today's job market, especially for knowledge workers.

The next unemployment release will be interesting, and if it rises, I will not be surprised. AI may be making some jobs, but it seems to be taking more of them, at least for now.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Walmart Q4 Results: A Rare Miss On Earnings Outlook, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Walmart’s (WMT) stock price is holding steady following the release of its Q4 results and associated outlook, which came in surprisingly below forecast. What is more surprising, perhaps, is the stability in the stock price despite the miss, especially given WMT’s sky-high valuation.

Overall sentiment in the stock is quite unsettled, with many calling shares in WMT a 'strong sell,' while others see it as a 'strong buy.'

From my lens, I believe existing shareholders would benefit from holding onto their shares, which likely are sitting on hefty gains. I’m neutral on the stock today, given current trading values, but I view the outlook as positive. I believe the Q4 results support my viewpoints here.

WMT Stock Key Metrics

Shares in WMT have gained over 170% over the last five years, and it’s been one of the best-performing stocks in the broader S&P 500 (SPY) during this period.

Part of WMT’s success has been its growth among seemingly every demographic of consumer, from low- to medium- to high income earners. Lately, it’s been the latter that has been the key demographic profile for WMT.

At any rate, the run-up in WMT’s stock price has driven its forward earnings multiple to nearly 50x. This is well above both its historical averages and the sector median. It’s no surprise, then, that WMT’s valuation has received a ‘F’ from Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant ratings.

The poor grading on valuations has dragged shares to an overall ‘hold’ rating, despite ‘A’ grades in three of the five rating categories.

What is surprising, in my view, is the deviation in sentiment between the SA analyst community and Wall Street. While SA analysts view the stock as a ‘sell,’ Wall Street is more bullish. The caveat, however, based on the latest price targets, is that it appears the upside is limited from here.

From my perspective, I'm neutral on the stock and would prefer to keep it on ‘hold’ as opposed to calling it an outright ‘sell.’

WMT Q4 Results Recap

WMT’s Q4 wasn’t the blowout investors have grown accustomed to, but it was far from weak.

At the topline, revenues rose 4.9% in constant currency, driven once again by eCommerce, which was up an impressive 24% globally. In the U.S., comps increased 4.6%, and online sales jumped 27%. In my view, the online sales growth shows just how critical digital has become to WMT’s overall growth story.

More to the bottom line, operating income grew 10.5%, more than double the pace of sales, marking the third straight year profits have outpaced revenue growth. It's worth noting that this was a key point emphasized by the management team on the earnings call.

One of the bigger highlights was mix. Higher-margin areas like advertising and membership income continue to scale, with the two accounting for nearly one-third of operating income in the quarter. Inventory was also well controlled, up just 2.6%, roughly half the rate of sales growth.

Higher-income shoppers were also deep in focus, with households earning over +$100K driving a majority of share gains, according to management’s commentary.

While the headline certainly included the miss on the profit outlook, the underlying trends suggest WMT’s outlook remains better than positive.

What Is The Outlook For WMT Stock?

Looking ahead to the new fiscal year, the guide is for 3.5% to 4.5% sales growth and operating income growth of 6% to 8%, with EPS between $2.75 and $2.85. That outlook came in a bit lighter than some analysts had hoped, which likely explains the muted reaction in shares.

Nonetheless, the framework implies continued margin expansion and profits growing faster than sales, a trend the company has now delivered on for three consecutive years.

Perhaps to no one’s surprise, e-commerce is expected to remain the primary growth driver, supported by advertising, membership income, and ongoing automation benefits.

Importantly, management emphasized conservatism in its guidance, citing tariff pressures, pharmacy pricing legislation, and a still-uncertain consumer environment. This could possibly set a low bar for success in Q1.

For WMT, the consumer picture remains bifurcated. Higher-income households continue to trade into WMT, while lower-income shoppers are feeling pressure from elevated prices and reduced government benefits. Grocery pricing remains relatively stable, but general merchandise inflation and tariff-related costs could create a headwind for the lower-income cohort.

In my view, WMT appears well-positioned operationally, but expectations remain elevated, and execution will need to stay tight under new CEO John Furner to maintain its current lofty valuation.

Is WMT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

There was a lot to like in the Q4 release. Digital growth remains strong, advertising and membership are scaling nicely, and operating income continues to grow faster than sales. The business model also looks more diversified and structurally stronger than it did even a few years ago.

At current prices, however, it’s hard to justify additional positioning into the stock, especially when the forward forecast came in lighter than expected.

Trading at roughly 50x forward earnings after a 170% five-year run, shares already reflect a great deal of optimism, in my view. Even with steady 3%–4% sales growth and mid-to-high single-digit operating income expansion, that multiple leaves limited room for error. Any stumble, particularly in this bifurcated consumer environment, could pressure the stock, in my view.

At the same time, I don’t see a compelling reason to sell if you’ve owned shares through this run. The company continues to take share, generate strong cash flow, and invest in long-term growth drivers.

For me, this is a high-quality company priced accordingly. That keeps WMT firmly in ‘hold’ territory at current levels.

data.title: Uber: Market Is Likely Pricing It Wrong By Ignoring Its Platform Moat
data.description:
Uber: Robotaxi Disruption Seems Like A Long Way Off

The market has truly turned very gloomy on the prospects of Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) in this autonomous driving race that could define the long term winners in the robotaxi era. Waymo’s (GOOGL) (GOOG) rapid scale-out and recent funding that elevated its valuation to 126 billion dollars has closed the gap with UBER’s valuation that’s just under $150B as I pen this update. Moreover, this has come at the time when UBER stock has already declined more than 30% through the lows last week. Therefore, I think the bifurcation cannot be even more stark, as the market is already trying to decide the ultimate winners in the race between Uber and big technology companies like Google’s Waymo and, of course, Tesla (TSLA).

As a reminder, in my previous Uber write-up, I decided to upgrade the stock to a buy, a view of what I thought was a more constructive risk/reward. However, the stock has continued to take a downturn, with the market still seemingly unconvinced with its robotaxi prospects.

The interesting thing is, it is not as if Uber’s current mobility and deliveries business model has fallen apart, because that is simply not the case, as we saw in the company's fourth quarter earnings release in early February. As you can probably observe from above, it's crystal clear that the key operating metrics and even gross bookings are still gaining traction confidently. And note that Uber stated that its MAPC base (monthly active consumers) of just over 200M is still less than 50% of its overall 450M annual active consumer base, suggesting there are plenty of opportunities for the company to ramp up its monetization.

Furthermore, the growth of its platform business has also expanded beyond the top 20 cities that could be maturing. And while these are highly profitable and are core revenue drivers for Uber, the growth profile has broadened and shifted into the last-dance cities and also into the international business that Uber has been extending its tentacles and clout into. Management noted these burgeoning opportunities, which should lend credence to more constructive investor sentiments, even though the recent price action has demonstrated investors are looking far beyond these positive developments, as they focus on how the robotaxi battle royale could shape up between Uber and the leading purveyors in the current landscape.

Uber's Platform Moat Remains Highly Formidable For AVs

I believe it is not a coincidence that management decided to preface its presentation during the earnings conference to discuss and emphasize the Uber advantage when it comes to robotaxi deployment. And Uber clearly wants to make a deep impression on the investor class as well as on Wall Street that the company isn't just committed but also owns a robust two-sided platform moat in driving growth for third party partners who are willing to partner with Uber in partaking in the nascent and fast growing robotaxi opportunity.

I think this clearly shows that the Uber platform has not been disrupted, and it's not even clear that it could be disrupted despite Waymo making credible gains in Uber's current market. And I think this explains why Waymo is leveraging Uber as a partner right now but also being keen and having the great ambition to become the dominant platform as it seeks to scale to 20+ cities in 2026 (including in ex-US markets), as compared to Uber’s estimated 15 cities by the end of 2026. In this early stage of the competition, scaling quickly will be one of the key outcomes that the investor class will be very keen to pay close attention to. And since Waymo has demonstrated its execution capabilities while also getting a much needed valuation uplift, the downside that Uber suffered does seem like a representation of the current market sentiments against its network economics from being replicated in the robotaxi era.

Interestingly, I think Uber management has been very upfront about the competitive dynamics of the robotaxi era, and the company hasn't downplayed the challenges emanating from its highly ambitious and well resourced archrivals. We know that Tesla appears to have started its Cybercab production, and its FSD revelations in the recent earnings call show clearly that the company is gaining traction not just in terms of subscriptions but also in terms of its technology capabilities.

And with Nvidia's (NVDA) AV tech stack also aligning with Tesla’s vision only approach, Waymo’s more costly business model (that also relies on multiple sensors such as radar, LiDAR, etc. to build in robust redundancy) could also be an impediment when considering the need to scale more cost efficiently when battling for market share in key growing markets.

Currently, there is no doubt that Waymo is in the driver’s seat and is gaining speed quickly. What is also clear is that Uber is not about to be disrupted in the next couple of years, and possibly more. Yet, I think the market is becoming less receptive to revaluing its multiples to even greater heights, unless they could have enough confidence that Uber’s asset-light model might not have to undergo significant changes, requiring the company to invest in increasingly asset heavy business strategies that could crimp its solidly profitable business model.

Although the company attempted to defend its status quo, particularly on the suite of partners in its AV ecosystem, the fact that Waymo could secure such strong recognition in its valuation is a testament to the market's confidence in Google's technology stack. And clearly Google's AI capabilities have been put to great use in helping Waymo train its edge cases so that they could become even more cost efficient and even safer in deployment. This could have great benefits for Waymo, considering that it wants to scale even more quickly. And its ability to gain traction against Uber in newer markets could have downside ramifications not only against Uber’s current business but could also compel Uber’s fledgling slate of AV partners to compete at a subscale level. And then, we also haven’t considered Tesla's expansion plans, which could intensify the misery that Uber investors have to deal with.

Wall Street Turning Cautious On Uber's Earnings Outlook

What truly bothers me this time is not that Uber will be disrupted in the next year, because I think that's obviously not going to happen. But Wall Street is also getting a lot less optimistic about Uber’s ability to scale its earnings outlook. As seen above, analysts are starting to ask more introspective questions that have led them to downgrade Uber’s earnings accretion. And since earnings growth is expected to be impacted, I believe it only makes sense for the market to start derating UBER’s bullish narrative.

In other words, while Uber is still highly free cash flow profitable and still expanding in its international markets while also growing its deliveries business, mobility is still the core revenue and adjusted EBITDA anchor. Note that mobility's adjusted EBITDA is still 2.2x that of its deliveries business in the past 12 months, making this segment not just important but also foundational in the company’s blueprint. I guess it possibly explains why the market seems a lot less certain about Uber’s long-term outlook amidst the glaring uncertainties emanating from its robotaxi capabilities and whether its current 3P, or third-party partners, are up to the task and scale to outcompete against Waymo and Tesla. While I believe the jury is still out, the market seems to have reached a verdict that unless Uber’s 3P partners could put up a more fearsome competition against Waymo and Tesla in the nascent AV markets, UBER’s valuation could have reached a near-term peak and could be mired in doldrums for some time.

Even though my commentary appears to have accorded a less optimistic assessment of Uber’s thesis, I must highlight that the current market sentiments as well as the valuation of UBER have also aptly considered these threats. Makes sense, right? Think about it. UBER lost more than 30% of its value through the lows last week. There appears to be some respite this week, as I think the market could be contemplating whether they have taken this hammering way too far.

Why is that so? Note that the company still owns a highly robust two-sided network, encompassing the consumer side as well as the supplier side. And the network effects that accrue to Uber allow them to expand into highly monetizable pillars like advertising and subscriptions. And consider that human drivers are still necessary to reach its higher growth areas, which are less dense and obviously not a key market for the robotaxis to compete in the near term; I think the market's verdict on Uber’s capabilities appears to be premature. By knocking down UBER’s valuation down to 22x, the stock’s valuation is also below its 1Y average of 23x, indicating that high conviction buyers could also see this decline as a remarkable opportunity to accumulate quickly.

For Uber, it is my intention to lead this discussion with the bear case first so that you can consider what I believe are the most prominent threats. Yet, at the same time, I think it's important for us to balance Uber’s response and show that its third party platform economics has not quite been decimated by the advances made by Waymo and Tesla.

What we have seen with Nvidia, I think it's pretty clear that Jensen Huang sees this opportunity to penetrate deeper into the autonomous driving tech stack and has also partnered with Uber by using Uber’s real world data to train its AV models. Hence Nvidia clearly sees a profound opportunity to partner with auto OEMs, AV suppliers, and even mobility platform operators like Uber to lower the astronomical cost curve that is necessary to build a world class AV tech stack that could be out of their abilities if they aren’t called Tesla or Waymo.

Therefore, I think the world should not come to a big and definitive conclusion yet that Uber is already out of the game, like what the market appears to be signaling. Because if it turns out that the robotaxi advances are slower than expected, it could help Uber better retain its market share while working out the network economics for the robotaxi deployment with its 3P partners. Also, it is not immediately clear that Tesla or Waymo have conclusively solved the robotaxi scaling game that could render other vendors irrelevant.

With that in mind, it does seem to me that the market has gone too quickly to invalidate UBER’s robust moat and sticky ecosystem that apparently is still making inroads across its core businesses right now, as highlighted earlier.

Buy Or Sell UBER Stock?

And also, if we look at UBER’s price action, it’s clear to me that the stock is still leaning on a long-term uptrend bias. While the multi-month decline has definitely weakened sentiments in the past few months, the extent of the decline also appears to be quite rapid. In other words, the market might have reached a capitulation bottom on UBER’s thesis, which could also have flushed out some of the optimism that undergirded its rise to the peak in September of 2025. Moreover, if you observe UBER’s momentum indicator, it has undoubtedly reached oversold levels for the first time since 2022. Previously, it almost reached those levels in July of 2024 and April of 2025, but it never clearly reached that oversold zone.

Therefore, I think the market has really taken this decline way too far, as those who bailed out couldn’t fathom the uncertainties of hanging on any longer to see Uber’s AV opportunity pan out. Well, I couldn't blame these investors, especially since there are tremendous uncertainties right in the AV market.

Yet, I think it's important for us to realize that UBER’s price action hasn’t quite lost its luster when juxtaposed with a longer-term view. So, while the market has decided to look elsewhere, I think Uber investors should relish this opportunity to accumulate.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Hartford Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The cooling labor market and worries about upside inflation risks also kept the markets on edge. In the third quarter, the US economy grew at the fastest pace in two years; GDP surged 4.3% annually, primarily fueled by robust consumer spending but also aided by a rebound in exports, business investment on equipment and AI, and increased government spending on defense. The Federal Reserve (Fed) reduced interest rates by 50 basis points (bps)* during the quarter. Policymakers were divided on their outlook for interest rates amid considerable uncertainty about inflation and the labor market, with the Fed's median dot-plot projection showing only one rate cut in 2026.

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford Equity Income Fund (I Share) underperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index during the quarter.
  • Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock-selection process, was the primary driver of relative underperformance. An underweight to communication services and overweight to utilities detracted from relative results, while our overweight to healthcare and underweight to consumer discretionary contributed, partially offsetting relative performance.
  • Stock selection contributed to relative performance. Strong selection in healthcare, materials, and energy was partially offset by weak selection in information technology, communication services, and utilities.
  • At the issuer level, our top-two relative detractors were not holding benchmark constituents Alphabet (communication services) and Micron Technology (information technology), while our top-two relative contributors were an overweight position in Merck (healthcare) and an out-of-benchmark position in Eli Lilly (healthcare).

Positioning & Outlook

  • As we look toward the end of the year, the large-cap universe is rich with new opportunities. We continue to be laser-focused on finding high-quality businesses with attractive valuation and capital returns. We take a balanced approach to portfolio construction—not leaning too heavily on any one scenario—and we always strive to upgrade the quality of the portfolio.
  • At the end of the period, our largest sector overweights were utilities, healthcare, and real estate. We were most underweight to communication services, financials, and information technology.

* Basis point is a unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1% and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security

data.title: POWR: This Power Infrastructure ETF Offers Long-Term Returns
data.description:
As the power demand is expected to boost in the years ahead, exploring opportunities in the power infrastructure market could be a prudent strategy. iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF (POWR) offers access to broader U.S. power infrastructure market, given its stake in power supply, generation, transmission, distribution, and storage companies from various sectors.

Year-to-date, POWR’s price momentum remained strong. Its dividend yield is also high, with the prospect of solid dividend growth in 2026 and ahead. The fund’s expense ratio and liquidity are in line with the median of all ETFs. Therefore, I initiate coverage of POWR with a buy rating.

US Power Demand Is Expected to Surge at a Record Pace

According to the Energy Information Administration data, the U.S. electricity demand increased in the past two years amid power demand from data centers, urbanization, and industrials sector. Moreover, the demand is expected to increase by 1% in 2026 and accelerate by 3% in 2027, marking the strongest four-year growth period since 2000.

On the other hand, an increased concentration on clean energy will enable solar electricity generation to attract massive investments. The agency expects 17% and 23% growth in solar generation in 2026 and 2027, respectively. Whereas, investments are also likely to increase in coal, nuclear, and hydroelectric projects to meet the demand. In the past two years, companies across the utilities sector announced hundreds of billions of dollars of investments in existing and new projects to prepare for potential demand growth.

Consequently, the business activities across the power infrastructure market are expected to boost ahead. Therefore, instead of investing in a single stock or utilities-focused ETFs, it might be a smart strategy to capitalize on the broader power infrastructure market. iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF could be one of the best investment vehicles to gain exposure to the broader power market.

POWR’s Diversified Portfolio Can Help Offer Solid Returns

iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF’s share price rallied more than 9% year-to-date, while its total returns hovered around 11.35%. Its price momentum and dividend returns are supported by its diversified exposure to power infrastructure market. In addition to the electricity producers, its portfolio offers exposure to power infrastructure-related companies from the industrials, energy, technology, and materials sectors. These stocks make up almost half of its portfolio weight.

From the utilities sector, POWR’s portfolio offers highest exposure to the fast-growing electric utilities, accounting for 36% of its portfolio weight. The electric utilities are gaining investors traction due to their potential to generate solid profits in the year ahead. Whereas multi-utilities make up 11% of its portfolio weight and independent power producers represent 3.5%.

NextEra (NEE), the electric utilities company, is its largest holding. NextEra’s share price soared 13% year-to-date. In addition, the company recently announced a 10% dividend hike. The company’s latest performance, outlook, and investment targets vindicate my opinion about power demand.

In 2025, the company’s adjusted earnings per share of $3.71 increased 8% from the year-ago period. Moreover, the company anticipates a compound annual adjusted earnings per share growth of more than 8% through 2032. It anticipates investment between $90 billion and $100 billion in the next six years through its Florida Power & Light unit.

POWR’s other top utilities companies are Southern Co. (SO), Duke Energy (DUK), Constellation Energy (CEG), American Electric Power (AEP), Dominion Energy (D), Vistra Corp. (VST), Exelon Corp. (EXC), XCEL Energy (XEL), and Entergy Corp. (ETR). All these companies have generated the solid returns in 2025 and so far in 2026.

POWR’s top stock holdings from industrials sector are Quanta Services (PWR), GE Vernova (GEV), Eaton Corp. (ETN), and Hubbell Incorporated (HUBB). These companies are involved in providing infrastructure solutions and electrical components and equipment to power companies.

For example, Quanta Services offers infrastructure solutions to utilities companies across the world. Its stock price rallied more than 80% in the last twelve months. In the latest quarter, it achieved double-digit revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted earnings per share growth from the previous year period.

GE Vernova’s shares soared more than 115% in the last twelve months amid the record financial numbers and backlog. The company is engaged in providing various products and services that generate, transfer, orchestrate, convert, and store electricity. In the latest quarter, its total backlog increased by more than 25% to $150 billion.

Hubbell Incorporated, which manufactures and sells electrical and utility solutions for grid modernization and electrification megatrends, is also among its top 10 holdings. The company’s stock price soared 18% year-to-date and 32% in the last twelve months. In the latest quarter, Hubbell delivered 12% revenue growth and 15% adjusted earnings per share growth, driven by the robust demand from data centers, grid investments, and load growth.

From the energy sector, POWR offers exposure to natural gas companies. In the US, the natural gas makes up the highest weight in total electricity generation. POWR’s top stocks from the energy sector are EQT Corp. (EQT), Expand Energy (EXE), Antero Resources (AR), Range Resources (RRC), and CNX Resources (CNX).

POWR’s Other Key Characteristics

In addition to the price momentum, iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF offers an impressive dividend yield of 6.5%, making it a solid candidate for the long-term investing. In the trailing twelve months, POWR lifted its dividend by 79%, while its past five years dividend growth CAGR was 28%. It paid dividends in the last 13 consecutive years. In 2026, I expect a solid dividend hike because of the strong earnings growth power of its portfolio holdings from the utilities and industrials sectors.

In addition, POWR trades in line with the S&P 500. Its trailing price-to-earnings ratio is around 28x, while its price-to-book ratio is 3.13. Its per share price of $26 also appears low. A low per share price can increase investors potential to buy the large number of shares. Besides that, the fund’s expense ratio of 0.40% is down slightly from the median of all ETFs, while liquidity is in line with the median of all ETFs.

Risk Factors to Consider

There are multiple risk factors to consider. iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF’s performance heavily depends on electricity demand. In the case of tighter regulation for data centers and electric vehicles, the power demand outlook can decline. In addition, economic trends could also negatively impact power demand. POWR’s standard deviation of 19 and annualized volatility of 21.6% are higher than the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

As the power sector is expected to experience a robust demand growth trend, activities in the power infrastructure market are likely to amplify significantly in the years ahead. Therefore, instead of investing only in power producers, it might be a great idea to chase the broader power infrastructure market. iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF offers access to selected power infrastructure companies from the utilities, industrials, energy, and technology sectors. POWR’s price momentum began to accelerate in 2026 with the prospect of hefty dividend growth.

data.title: Dell Technologies Q4 Earnings Preview: Sustaining Growth With AI Momentum
data.description:
Setting The Stage

As I cover Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL), it is gearing up for its Q4 2026 and FY2026 earnings release scheduled for 26th February, 2026. This is a diversified IT infrastructure company spanning AI-optimized service, storage, PCs, and enterprise solutions. The company has transitioned from a legacy PC powerhouse to a key beneficiary of the AI infrastructure boom, specifically through its Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG).

Its shares are trading at about $116.78, almost muted over the last 1 year, losing about 3% and mostly performing within range with the S&P 500. Its muted performance is an indication of weak momentum due to the broader AI hardware market rotation.

With this performance, Q4 2026 proves to be decisive as we assess whether it could reignite momentum and provide a bullish FY2027 visibility. Despite the weak stock momentum, DELL has demonstrated solid financial performance anchored on AI adoption, as shown by its solid double-digit growth in its ISG segment. As such, I think investors should seize the broader market weakness to ride on this AI boom before the stock starts soaring.

Recapping Recent Performance: AI Acceleration Fueling Growth

In Q3 2026, DELL delivered a standout performance with a record Q3 revenue of $27 billion, up by about 11% YoY. This marked the seventh consecutive quarter of positive revenue growth and the first consecutive double-digit quarterly revenue growth in over 15 quarters. In my view, this is a demonstration of building revenue momentum driven by strong AI adoption. For example, the company reported an all-time high in AI server orders of $12.3 billion in Q3 2026, taking the YTD orders to about $30 billion.

To add perspective here, the company’s ISG segment remains the main growth powerhouse, with its Q3 revenue surging by 24% YoY to $14.1 billion, driven by servers & networking, which grew by 37% YoY. It is important to note that the ISG segment has sustained double-digit growth for about 7 consecutive quarters compared to the modest single-digit growth in the Client Solution Group (CSG) segment, validating my assertion that this segment is the key growth driver. In my view, the volatility in the segment's growth rate reflects the cyclical nature of the AI infrastructure market.

Looking at the projected 25.7% CAGR in the AI infrastructure market, I see a bright outlook for DELL’s growth engine.

This coincides with the company’s record AI server order backlog of $18.4 billion in Q3. To me, this signifies that the ISG growth momentum is likely to be sustained in the long run, which is a reason to be bullish on this stock.

On the profitability front, the company’s Q3 2026 net income came in at $1.55 billion, a 32% YoY growth and a consecutive quarter of more than 31% YoY growth. This translated to a diluted EPS of $2.28, marking a YoY growth of about 39% and a consecutive quarter of more than 38% YoY diluted EPS growth.

In my view, this strong profitability shows that the company is translating its top-line growth into strong bottom-line momentum, which solidifies its fundamentals. When I look at the above metrics, I believe that this stock’s lack of momentum isn’t tied to its fundamental weakness but rather to the external market tailwinds, such as the sentiments around the AI bubble.

However, given the company’s solid backlog, the double-digit projected AI infrastructure market growth, and the increasing adoption of AI, I believe the bubble fears would be short-lived, and we’re likely to see a solid uptrend in this stock just like other AI stocks dwarfed by these fears.

Q4 2026 Expectations & Focus Areas

In the previous quarter, the management guided a Q4 2026 revenue of $31-32 billion, which would be about 32% YoY growth at midpoint. On the bottom line, the non-GAAP EPS guidance stands at $3.5, a significant leap from Q4 2025 diluted EPS of $2.32. Now, comparing this to the consensus estimates, I see a close alignment, which is an indication of shared optimism.

For example, the consensus revenue estimate for the quarter is $31.65 billion, a 32.25%, which is close to the guided range. This implies that DELL is likely to continue its strong double-digit revenue growth in the upcoming quarter, which further aligns with my bullish take. Further, the consensus EPS of $3.52 translates to a YoY of 31.51%, which is in line with the current EPS momentum of about 31% YoY growth over the last two quarters. This further signals a potential solid profitability momentum in the coming quarter.

Given the company’s solid demand for its AI solutions, such as the $15.6 billion AI server shipments in Q3 2026 and the robust backlog, I believe the company’s estimated growth trajectory is plausible and sustainable. As such, I expect a strong Q4 characterized by strong top- and bottom-line growth.

As we eagerly wait for the quarterly report, I have several areas of focus that I would recommend for investors. First off, we should pay close attention to the AI server execution and backlog conversion. With about $18.4 billion in backlog, watch for confirmation of about $9.4 billion in Q4 shipments. Anything short of this would signal a low conversion rate, which could be a sign of weak execution. Further, look for updates on any upward revisions for the FY2027 AI outlook, as this could be a major catalyst.

Another area of focus that I will recommend is the CSG/PC recovery. While AI dominates this company’s growth, CSG has shown sequential strength with the 4th straight quarter of positive growth, although modest. Any signs of a broader PC refresh cycle or share gains would add ballast.

My last area of interest is the management’s guidance on FY2027. I think this could be one of the main movers. Any commentary on overall revenue growth potentially in the mid-teens and above and margin expansions might ignite a bullish run.

Valuation

Given the company’s strong revenue growth, I will value it based on a comparative P/S approach. Compared to its peers HP Inc. (HPQ) and International Business Machines Corporation (IBM), I tend to believe DELL is undervalued. With a P/S of 0.78x, DELL trails the peer’s average P/S of 1.99x.

When I look at this valuation alongside the revenue growth, it is a clear undervaluation. With a trailing revenue growth of 10.79% YoY, DELL beats the peer’s average growth of 5.42%, given HPQ’s 3.24% and IBM’s 7.62%. Looking at it from the forward perspective, it's even more interesting. DELL has a forward revenue growth of 12.22%, which is not only above HPQ’s 1.77% and IBM’s 5.77% but also the only upgrade from the three companies’ trailing revenue growth rates. In my view, this shows that DELL has a strong revenue growth potential compared to its peers, and as such, I believe it deserves a rerating. In my view, a P/S in the region of 3x-5x is admissible.

Risks

Although I am bullish on this stock, there lies a potential risk of margin compression due to rising memory/component costs. Although the company responded with price hikes in December, meaning it is pushing the cost burden to its consumers, this could lead to a softer demand, which could affect both the top and bottom line. Investors should keep a close eye on any updates as far as pricing and margins, specifically for DRAM, NAND, and hard drives, which are the most affected, and the impact the already effected price changes could have on demand, growth, and margins.

Conclusion

The Q4 2026 earnings report and FY2027 outlook present a critical test of whether a bullish momentum would be reignited or not. For long-term investors, the AI tailwind remains intact, and DELL’s diversified portfolio (servers, storage, PCs, etc.) offers resilience. In my view, short-term weak stock momentum appears overdone given guidance strength and backlog visibility. I believe a strong delivery and forward-positive commentary could ignite a meaningful re-rating towards higher multiples. For these reasons, I am confident in the company’s fundamentals, and as such, I rate it a buy.

Bond funds entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds advanced 1.10% in the fourth quarter of 2025, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. This result capped a year of elevated volatility, yet one in which the bond market posted gains in all four quarters. For the full year, the Aggregate index rose 7.30%, its best annual result since 2020.

Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals. The U.S. Federal Reserve, after resuming the monetary easing program it had paused from January until September of this year, followed through with additional policy rate reductions in October and December. The three rate cuts in 2025's second half, each of a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), brought the federal funds rate down to a target range of 3.5% to 3.75%, from a peak range of 5.25% to 5.5%, which the central bank maintained from July 2023 to September 2024.

The reductions drove short-term bond yields lower and contributed to gains for the investment-grade bond market in October and November (both +0.62%). Following its December meeting, though, Fed officials signaled they might hold steady for now. With progress on inflation stalled – monthly metrics continued to show U.S. consumer inflation moving within a tight range around 3%, above the Fed's 2% target – and labor-market statistics softening but remaining relatively resilient, Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated a wait-and-see stance and said, "We're well-positioned to see how the economy evolves from here."

This announcement shifted the market's expectation for additional rate cuts deeper into 2026, and the Aggregate index took a small step back in December (-0.15%).

For the three months, short-term bond yields fell in step with the benchmark interest rate, but long-term yields rose marginally, a function of the Fed's policy signals as well as heavy issuance of long- term Treasurys to fund U.S. government spending.

Due to this steepening of the yield curve, short- and intermediate- term issues fared best in Q4. Bonds with maturities of 7 to 10 years posted the strongest advance at 1.52%, while 1- to 3-year bonds rose 1.18%, 3- to 5-year maturities were up 1.32%, and 5- to 7-year bonds climbed 1.36%. Conversely, long-term securities of 10+ years were weakest (-0.00%). By credit rating, higher-quality bonds rated AAA and AA outperformed investment-grade debt rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

At the sector level, U.S. Treasurys (+0.90%) slightly outperformed investment-grade corporate bonds (+0.84%), which were constrained by historically tight credit spreads. (Notably, for the full year, corporates advanced 7.77%, comfortably besting the 6.38% gain for Treasurys). Other yield-advantaged, credit-sensitive sectors – including agency mortgage-backed securities (+1.71%), commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.34%) and asset-backed securities (+1.25%) – exhibited relative strength in Q4, while government-related bonds (+1.12%) finished roughly in line with the broader market.

Outside the Aggregate index, returns varied widely. U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained just 0.13%, per Bloomberg. But investor interest in higher-yielding assets drove strong results for higher-income categories. U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+1.31%) outperformed investment-grade corporates, while high-yield emerging-markets debt (4.85%) delivered the bond market's best return. For the year, investment-grade and high-yield emerging- markets bonds led all categories, gaining 9.45% and 13.93%, respectively, aided by improving fundamentals and a weaker U.S. dollar.■

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment

Fixed-income assets advanced modestly in Q4. Most yield-advantaged sectors outperformed U.S. Treasurys, particularly in the short-duration part of the curve.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Overall, sector positioning contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Year Government/ Credit Bond Index.

  1. Overweighting U.S. corporate bonds aided relative performance. (Positive)
  2. Non-benchmark allocations to asset-backed securities, mortgage-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities each also helped. (Positive)
  3. In contrast, an underweight stake in government-related bonds detracted from the fund's relative result. (Negative)

Strategy: Security Selection

Market Environment

By term, intermediate bonds with maturities of 7 to 10 years had the strongest gain. By credit rating, higher-quality bonds rated AAA and AA outperformed lower-quality investment-grade debt.

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)

Security selection among investment-grade corporate bonds added relative value the past three months.

  • Within the industrial sector, picks among consumer cyclical and consumer non-cyclical corporates contributed. (Positive)

Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve

Market Environment

The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened in Q4, with bond yields falling at the front end of the curve and rising at the long end.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

The fund's yield-curve positioning detracted.

  • The fund was underweight one-year maturities in favor of two- and three-year positions. (Negative)
  • The fund's overall duration was generally in line with that of the benchmark. (Neutral)

Outlook and Positioning

At the end of 2025, the global and U.S. business cycles remained constructive, with U.S. monetary and fiscal easing likely to continue in 2026. U.S. trends included strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and to date a resilient consumer sector.

Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields ended Q4 effectively unchanged from the previous quarter at roughly 4.2%, after beginning the year around 4.6%. The Fed eased monetary policy in the second half of 2025, cutting its short-term policy rate by 75 basis points and ending quantitative tightening (after reducing its balance sheet by $2.5 trillion since 2022).

The market currently anticipates two more Fed rate reductions in 2026, based on expectations for weakening employment trends and near-term inflation, though those cuts may not come until mid-year or later. The yield curve steepened in 2025 amid concerns over fiscal deficits, persistent inflation and Fed independence, all of which pressured long-term yields.

As of December 31, about 36% of the fund's assets were invested in corporate bonds. Within corporates, financials remained our largest sector allocation, at roughly 22% of total assets. The portfolio also owned about 42% U.S. Treasurys and a 16% non-benchmark allocation to asset-backed securities, including securitized car loan debt and collateralized loan obligations. Consistent with our strategy, the fund was notably underweight Treasurys, which represented approximately 72% of the benchmark at year-end.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market, based on our view of pricing and fundamentals. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management.

It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.■

data.title: Single-Family And Multifamily Construction: Bring On The Supply Just As Population Growth Slows To A Crawl
data.description:
This is precisely what this overpriced and frozen housing market needs.

In 2025, construction was started on 1.36 million privately owned housing units, down just a hair from a year ago: 943,000 single-family housing units and 416,000 multifamily housing units (condos and rental apartments), according to Census Bureau data today.

About 2.3 people occupy a housing unit on average. So the 1.36 million housing units that were started in 2025 would provide a home, when completed, for 3.1 million additional people. But the US population increased by only 1.78 million people in the 12 months through mid-2025; and for the 12 month through mid-2026, it is projected to grow by only 756,000 people; and growth might further decline in the following years, according to the Census Bureau’s estimates.

In the years before 2009, the US population grew by 2.5-2.8 million people per year. In the 12 months through mid-2025, population growth was half that. For the 12 months through mid-2026, population growth will be between one-third to one-quarter of that. This continued flood of new housing supply amid dramatically slowing population growth is precisely what this overpriced and frozen housing market needs.

Single-family housing units

Construction starts of privately owned single-family housing units, at 943,000 in 2025, were down by 6.9% from 2024 but roughly on the same pace as in 2023, as homebuilders pulled back amid very high inventories of new homes for sale and falling prices.

Falling prices of new single-family homes: For example, Lennar (LEN), one of the largest homebuilders in the US, has been trying to maintain its sales volume by piling on incentives, cutting prices, giving up a substantial part of its big-fat pandemic-era profit margins, and building at lower costs.

As a result, the average price of the homes that it sold has plunged; and for Q1 2026, it guided the average price down further, to a range of $365,000-375,000 net of incentives. The mid-point ($370,000 blue in the chart) would be the lowest sales price since Q1 2017, and down by 25% from the peak in Q3 2022 ($491,000).

Soaring inventories of new single-family homes: Inventories of completed single-family homes for sale by homebuilders have piled up to the highest level since 2009, to 124,000, per the Census Bureau’s latest data available through October. And homebuilders are responding to it with lower average selling prices. Bring on the supply!

Multifamily housing units

Construction starts of privately owned multifamily housing units (rentals and condos in buildings of 2 or more units) rose by 17.4% to 416,000 units in 2025.

Outside of the years 2021-2023, the 2025 pace was the highest since 1986. Bring on the supply!

In many markets, there has been a flood of higher-end rental apartments and condos coming on the market. The bigger projects take years to plan and build. Vacancies have been rising as these units came on the market. Many projects that had been planned but not started became stalled due to lack of financing as the CRE depression spread around. Asking rents have been edging down in some of those markets (but rising in others). There have already been a number of large multifamily defaults, and the multifamily CMBS delinquency rate has surged to over 7%, from 1% in 2024.

About 95% of these multifamily housing units were in buildings with 5 or more units. Only about 5% were in small buildings with 2-4 units.

Construction starts of housing units in small multifamily buildings with 2-4 units accounted for just 5% of all multifamily construction starts, at 19,100 starts in 2025.

But the pace has accelerated by 66% since 2015 (11,500 starts), sort of a rising-from-the-ashes moment, after that segment had nearly vanished in 2008. This is a playground that is accessible to mom-and-pop developers and landlords.

In densely populated urban cores, higher-end multifamily housing units (condos and apartments) are about the only type of housing that is getting built.

With higher-end apartments, builders are targeting “renters of choice” - people who could afford to buy a house out in the suburbs, but want to live in a modern high-rise in a central location with all the amenities and conveniences, a short commute, possibly an easy social life, and the feeling of being in the middle of things, topped off by the flexibility that rentals provide.

Developers cannot target lower-income tenants or buyers because the construction costs just don’t pencil out, unless the buildings are subsidized - and there is that too.

Renters with less-stellar incomes generally have to make do with renting apartments or condos in older buildings in less desirable areas, sometimes in run-down buildings with five decades of deferred maintenance.

Single-family construction has been spreading further out from urban cores - the urban sprawl that is everywhere. This includes “build to rent” single-family developments, designed and built specifically as rentals mostly for “renters of choice” by large single-family landlords or by builders that then fill the developments with tenants and sell the income-producing project to large landlords, pension funds, and other funds.

These build-to-rent developments have common amenities, a leasing and maintenance office, and are more efficient to manage for the landlord than individual houses scattered across their market, and they’ve been selling those scatter units here and there at sky-high profits for the past two years. Read... The Biggest Single-Family Rental Landlords and Multifamily Landlords in the US: Big Shifts Underway

data.title: Blackstone Mortgage Trust's Dividend Yield Nears Double Digits As Impairments Dip
data.description:
Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) is paying out a well-covered near double-digit dividend yield from an aggressively diversified global commercial real estate credit portfolio that is seeing both revenue and distributable earnings strength. BXMT is huge, with its credit portfolio holding a principal balance of $18.16 billion as of the end of its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter. This was spread across 131 loans, with a maximum 2.5-year maturity and an all-in yield of 3.39%. The dividend is the prize, and BXMT last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.47 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.88 per share annualized for a 9.64% dividend yield.

BXMT's bull case is built around its incredibly well-diversified CRE portfolio, with around 48% of its portfolio collateral as of the end of its fourth quarter coming from non-U.S. markets. This was further diversified across asset classes, with Office properties forming 27% of the portfolio, Multifamily at 26%, and Industrial at 24%. U.S. CRE exposure is further broadly diversified across states, with California its largest exposure at 10% of the portfolio. This depth of diversification acts as a healthy hedge to a CRE market that's been discombobulated by stubbornly high long-term U.S. Treasury rates in light of Fed rate cuts.

The mREIT reported fourth-quarter total revenue of $159.3 million, up 39.2% over $114.45 million in its year-ago comp. The jump in revenue was mainly driven by a 538% increase in revenue from owned real estate, negating what was a 17.37% year-over-year dip in net income from loans to $83.92 million from $101.56 million. Bears would be right to point out that this jump in real estate owned income reflects BXMT taking control of the collateral on credit that's turned sour, and that the dip in net income from its credit portfolio reflects a setback in the mREIT's ability to maintain its dividend distribution at its current level. This bearish risk is heightened by total expenses that jump by 94% year-over-year to $108.06 million, driven by expenses from owned real estate.

Dividend Coverage, Balance Sheet Health, And Credit Quality

GAAP net income came in at $0.24 per share, up 3 cents from the year-ago comp. However, distributable earnings were negative at $2.07 per share after charge-offs, and $0.51 per share after adjusting for charge-offs. This means the mREIT is covering its dividend by 108.5%. The level of coverage improved sequentially with distributable earnings prior to charge-offs expanding from $0.48 per share in the third quarter and by 20% from the first quarter of 2025. BXMT cut its dividend by 24.2% in the summer of 2024, a move that shareholders would be keen to avoid happening again. Total returns for the mREIT are running at 4.77% over the last 1 year, slightly underperforming the 6.20% total return from the iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (REM). BXMT's dividend yield has trended lower from a peak of north of 14% in 2023 to its current range. This reflects material progress by the mREIT in addressing impaired loans and the perception of improving CRE credit quality headwinds.

The mREIT's loan portfolio was 99% performing as of the end of the fourth quarter, closing $7 billion of total investments in 2025, with a focus on multifamily and industrial loans at 85% of this total. The mREIT held a current expected credit loss reserve ("CECL") of $284.44 million at the end of the fourth quarter, down from $733.94 million a year ago. Crucially, BXMT's loan portfolio performance was up a full 400 basis points from its first quarter, with impaired loans down 96% from a peak of $2.3 billion in the third quarter of 2024 to $100 million as of the end of the fourth quarter. The mREIT also held cash and cash equivalents of $452.53 million at the end of the fourth quarter, up roughly 40% from its year-ago comp.

This improvement in its underlying portfolio has been driven by a pivot towards Multifamily and Industrial CRE, as well as taking ownership of impaired loans. BXMT is managed by a subsidiary of Blackstone (BX), one of the largest alternative asset managers, with around $1.3 trillion in assets under management. The mREIT reported a fourth quarter book value of $20.75 per share, a figure that includes $1.76 per share of CECL reserves and $0.47 per share of accumulated depreciation of owned real estate. This means the common shares at $19.51 per share are swapping hands at a small 6% discount to book value per share as of the end of the fourth quarter.

Conclusion

The mREIT's current dividend yield provides a 554 basis point spread to the U.S. 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) at 4.10%. This is around 41% above the mREIT's tightest level and comes with an improving credit quality profile. The dividend is also safe at its current level. I am rating BXMT a Buy.

data.title: ETHA Is Not Ethereum: The Structural Cost Of Owning The Wrapper
data.description:
Introduction

Owning the iShares Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHA) is different from owning Ethereum itself (Note: I will refer to Ethereum as ETH since that is the native crypto symbol for it. This should not be confused with the Mini ETF, whose ticker is ETH). The difference is not trivially one of convenience and custody. It is much more structural. Not only does ETHA package ETH inside a brokerage wrapper, but it also transforms a protocol asset into a regulated financial instrument. This changes many things.

Ethereum is a network asset. You can trade Ethereum 24/7, all day. ETH does proof-of-stake (PoS) validation. Its return has two components: what it gains from price appreciation and the PoS yield.

ETHA, on the other hand, is a Delaware trust. It holds ETH, and returns come only from price performance, after expenses. Like any normal market security, it trades only during market hours. There’s a 0.25% sponsor fee, and ETHA has neither any distributable income nor is involved in staking. It tracks the CME CF Ether-Dollar Reference Rate—New York Variant, a regulated benchmark rate.

I mentioned staking, but the differences go beyond that. These differences impact where returns come from. As we will also discuss shortly, they have an effect on volatility, counterparty exposure, and how productive capital remains when idle. If you are a direct ETH investor, you get both risks and rewards at the protocol level. You get no protocol participation risks if you are owning ETHA, but you get other benefits. These include benefits due to ETHA’s custodial structure, authorized participants, involvement of regulators in its oversight, and basic brokerage access.

Return Composition: Price-Only vs. Network-Productive Capital

One key way ETH is more productive than ETHA is because it does network validation under proof-of-stake. This produces a reward in token for its holder. So you are not just sitting and waiting for price appreciation—as ETHA does—but you are actually working your investment. ETHA doesn’t do this because it wants to remain within a strict regulatory framework. It also wants to avoid certain operational and legal risks. These risks are things like a strict lock-up period for ETH, slashing risks, and so on that hurt ETH’s liquidity. So ETHA relies only on price appreciation for its returns.

ETHA sponsor fees came to $11.84 million in the first nine months of 2025. That is against an AUM of $6.4 billion. That said, those fees do come out of the trust’s ether holdings, so, over time, they reduce NAV.

ETH itself is highly volatile, and price appreciation of 70-100% in a year could occur. If ETH goes up 80% in a year, a 0.25% fee and even the staking yield could be marginal relative to those gains. An investor wanting to avoid the complexity of direct Ethereum investing may still be able to successfully participate in its price gains by simply holding ETHA. However, in flat markets, the fee starts to bite.

So, say in a flat market ETH only goes up 6% annually, and the staking yield is 3%; then a direct staker is making 9% before costs. ETHA’s return during the same period would be 6% minus the fees, or 5.75%. So the annual spread is 3.25%, which could become a substantial difference over longer periods.

Tax Structure: Income Recognition vs. Deferred Realization

There are key differences in how the taxman treats these two sources of returns. Staking rewards of direct ETH holders are treated as ordinary income. So, whether you sell ETH or not, you pay taxes. Longer-term appreciation from those rewarded tokens becomes capital gains, so you pay capital gains tax on those too.

For ETHA holders, the tax situation is different. The ETF does not distribute income and does not stake. So you pay no periodic taxes. You only pay taxes when you sell ETHA. Until then, gains or losses are unrealized.

In the earlier example, only the staking portion of the 3.25% pre-tax spread is taxed annually, depending on your tax bracket. If you are paying 24% taxes, the spread comes to 2.53%. At 37%, it comes to 2.14%. This is still a gap, but it isn’t as much as earlier.

So we see deferral has some economic value for taxable investors. For retirement accounts, however, tax-sheltered accounts paying 0% taxes on income, the advantage disappears, and ETH becomes more rewarding.

Liquidity & Market Structure: 24/7 Protocol Asset vs. Exchange-Hour Instrument

Ethereum trades across many exchanges around the world. It goes up and down in real time. Unlike ETHA, it does not keep US market hours only. When US markets close, ETHA trading stops, but ETH trading doesn’t. This is a crucial difference.

So if you are holding ETHA, and the underlying ETH makes major weekend moves, you can’t get into the game until Monday. This happens routinely—crypto is notorious for making sharp moves outside US trading hours.

Another point: while ETHA does have a large asset base of between $6 billion and $9 billion (the difference is based on how it is being reported), ETH trades across dozens of global exchanges with $19-20 billion in daily turnover. That’s a 2-3x multiple of ETHA.

While these are constraints on ETHA, there are some benefits to them. One of these is that during trading hours, bid-ask spreads are tighter for ETHA. This helps brokerage investors with more predictable execution and settlement.

Counterparty & Custody Risk: Protocol Risk vs. Institutional Risk

Native ETH has various risks peculiar to protocol-level assets. Investors need to manage private keys, which could cause losses, especially if an asset is self-custodial. Unintentional improper validator behaviour could make you lose rewards. Intentional or malicious behaviour, although rare, could cause slashing penalties.

Another type of protocol-level risk is exposure to smart contracts. Here, a software bug could exploit drained funds. In some situations, especially outside the secure ether ecosystem, you could even see the staking pool contract getting compromised.

In a regulated setup like ETHA, these risks do not exist. Here, your ETF’s ether is held directly by a custodian (here, Coinbase Prime), so there are institutional safeguards. However, risks are not eliminated entirely; they just shift somewhere else. Here, with ETHA, you must rely on sponsors, custodians, and authorized participants. Each of these elements must work properly in the capital market for the account to clear and settle correctly.

Capital Efficiency & Financial Integration: Brokerage Asset vs. On-Chain Asset

Ethereum is an asset that can be made to work. It can be productive. You can deploy it in a decentralized manner and use it as collateral in network lending protocols. It can be added to liquidity pools or staked. You can basically use your owned on-chain ETH to earn more ETH.

This is not something you can do with ETHA. You do hold beneficial ownership in the trust that holds ETH, but what you own is not ETH but this trust. You cannot use those shares within decentralized systems. You cannot stake them, lend them on-chain, or deploy them in smart contracts.

On the other hand, you can margin ETHA on brokerage accounts, you can use it as collateral for ordinary loans, and there’s a busy option market so you can write covered calls, buy protective puts, and so on. In short, you can do financial engineering with the ETH-backed ETF that you couldn’t standardly do with native ETH (there are non-standard ways to do some of these things).

So it is not a simple trade-off on yield. You are also exchanging on-chain deployability for the flexibility of brokerage holding.

Conclusion

The foregoing discussion should have made clear who should go native with ETH and who should stick to ETHA. Broadly, there are three situations where owning ETH is more profitable. One, if you are highly tech-savvy and know how to go on the network, manage private keys, and stake your coins, ETH is for you. Two, if you are putting your money in tax-sheltered accounts, ETHA offers no tax advantage but only the fee drag. Third, if ether is going through a sustained flat market, then the price advantage of ETH outweighs the structural advantages of ETHA. In all these situations, owning ETH is the better deal.
